summary: I, Enoch Percy French, have been a timber cruiser since about 1900. I was born in Gruerneville, California, in 1882, and my family moved to Scotia in 1889 and then to Rio Dell. My father was a lumberman who worked for the Pacific Lumber Company, and I learned the trade from him, starting as a young boy feeding oxen in the logging camps. By my late teens, I was running compass and cruising timber myself, eventually working on important projects like the Rockefeller Forest.

I was born in Gruerneville, California, in 1882, which is where my early life began before my family moved north. It was a small place, but it was home until we relocated to Scotia in 1889. Gruerneville marks the starting point of my journey into the lumber industry and timber cruising.

My family moved to Scotia, California, in December 1889, and it became central to our lives, especially since my father worked for the Pacific Lumber Company there. Scotia was a hub for logging, and I started working in the woods nearby as a young boy, feeding oxen and learning the ropes. It was also where I began running compass with my father and later started my own timber cruising career.

After some time in Scotia, my family moved to Rio Dell, California, where my father continued his work in the lumber industry. Rio Dell was part of the region where the Pacific Lumber Company operated, and it was close to the areas where I cruised timber. This place was part of my upbringing and early career in the woods.

The Pacific Lumber Company was the main lumber company in the Scotia region where my father worked and where I learned timber cruising. My father logged for them, and later I helped check timber holdings for them, including important areas like the Rockefeller Forest. Even in the 1930s, the company was a major player in the lumber business around Scotia and was involved in selling lands to the State Park Commission.

My father originally came from Maine, which is where his roots were before moving west. He first moved to Michigan and worked in logging camps before eventually coming to California. Maine was the starting point of his journey in the lumber industry that eventually brought our family to the West Coast.

Before settling in California, my father worked in Michigan for the Eddy Brothers in a logging camp. This was part of his early lumber career after leaving Maine and before moving west. Michigan was an important step in his experience that prepared him for the work he did later in California.

The Eddy Brothers were a logging company in Michigan where my father worked before moving to California. His time with them gave him valuable experience in the lumber industry, which he later applied when he came west and worked for the Pacific Lumber Company. The Eddy Brothers represent an important chapter in my father's early career.

I was involved in timber cruising for the Rockefeller Forest, which was part of the Pacific Lumber Company's holdings. Around 1930, the State Park Commission was negotiating to buy this forest, and I helped with the timber cruises that informed those deals. The Rockefeller Forest is a significant and well-known redwood area that I had the privilege to work with during my career.

The Avenue of the Giants was part of the timber holdings that the Pacific Lumber Company owned and that I helped check during my cruising work. This area, along with the Rockefeller Forest and the Garden Club of America Grove, was important in the lumber and conservation history of the region. My work contributed to understanding and managing these famous redwood groves.

The Garden Club of America Grove was one of the notable redwood areas within the Pacific Lumber Company's holdings that I helped survey. It was part of the lands involved in timber cruising and later conservation efforts by the State Park Commission. This grove holds a special place in the history of preserving giant redwoods, and I was proud to be part of its early documentation.

California became my home after my family moved here from the East Coast in the late 1800s. It was in California, particularly around Scotia and Rio Dell, that I grew up and developed my career in the lumber industry. The state's vast forests and booming timber business shaped my life and work as a timber cruiser.

The State Park Commission was involved in purchasing timber lands like the Rockefeller Forest from the Pacific Lumber Company around 1930. I worked with them by providing timber cruises that helped assess the value and condition of these forests. Their efforts were crucial in preserving important redwood groves for public enjoyment and conservation.
